docs.title: 3. Initialization Sequence — init()
docs.text:
@startuml
participant ImuModule   as imu
participant TwoWire     as wire
participant "Adafruit\nICM20948" as lib
participant "Arduino\nGPIO"     as gpio

imu  -> gpio : pinMode(GPIO12, OUTPUT)
imu  -> gpio : digitalWrite(GPIO12, LOW)  ' AD0 = LOW → address 0x68
imu  -> wire : _wire1.begin()
imu  -> wire : _wire1.setClock(400000)

imu  -> lib  : begin_I2C(0x68, &_wire1)
alt sensor found
    lib --> imu : true
    imu -> lib  : setAccelRange(±4 g)
    imu -> lib  : setGyroRange(±500 dps)
    imu -> lib  : setMagDataRate(10 Hz)
    imu -> imu  : _ready = true\n_error = OK
    imu -> imu  : Serial.println("[IMU] OK")
else not found
    lib --> imu : false
    imu -> imu  : _error = NOT_FOUND\n_ready = false
    imu -> imu  : Serial.println("[IMU] not found")
end
@enduml

docs.title: 4. Measurement Sequence — read()
docs.text:
@startuml
participant ImuModule        as imu
participant "Adafruit\nICM20948" as lib
participant "g_adcs.imu"     as data

imu -> lib  : getEvent(&accel, &gyro, &temp, &mag)
note right of lib
  I2C1 burst read (400 kHz):
  · 14 bytes accel+gyro+temp
  · 8 bytes mag (AK09916)
  · float conversion ×9 axes
  NOP library ≈ 76 ops
end note
lib --> imu : sensors_event_t filled

imu -> imu  : amag² = ax²+ay²+az²

alt amag² == 0.0  [failed read sentinel]
    imu -> imu  : _error = DATA_INVALID\n_failCount++
    imu -> data : valid = false
    imu --> imu : return false

    alt _failCount >= 3
        imu -> imu : ' watchdog notified via sendStatus() in main
    end

else valid data
    imu -> imu  : _failCount = 0\n_error = OK
    imu -> data : ax/ay/az = accel  [m/s²]
    imu -> data : gx/gy/gz = gyro   [rad/s]
    imu -> data : mx/my/mz = mag    [µT]
    imu -> data : valid = true
    imu --> imu : return true
end
@enduml

docs.title: 5. Sensor Configuration
docs.text: Parameter Value Register constant Accelerometer range ±4 g ICM20948_ACCEL_RANGE_4_G Gyroscope range ±500 dps ICM20948_GYRO_RANGE_500_DPS Magnetometer rate 10 Hz AK09916_MAG_DATARATE_10_HZ I2C address 0x68 IMU_I2C_ADDR (config.h) I2C frequency 400 kHz IMU_I2C_FREQ (config.h)

docs.title: 6. Output — g_adcs.imu
docs.text: Field Unit Source axis ax, ay, az m/s² ICM-20948 accelerometer gx, gy, gz rad/s ICM-20948 gyroscope mx, my, mz µT AK09916 magnetometer valid bool false if read fails or amag² = 0

docs.title: 7. Attitude Fusion (future)
docs.text:
@startuml
component "Gyroscope\n(rad/s)" as GYR
component "Accelerometer\n(m/s²)" as ACC
component "Magnetometer\n(µT)" as MAG
component "Fusion Algorithm\n(TBD)" as FUSE
component "Quaternion\nq = [w, x, y, z]" as QUAT

GYR --> FUSE : angular rate\n(high-freq propagation)
ACC --> FUSE : gravity vector\n(pitch/roll correction)
MAG --> FUSE : Earth field\n(yaw correction)
FUSE --> QUAT

note bottom of FUSE
  Algorithm TBD after sensor characterization:
  · Complementary filter
  · Madgwick
  · Mahony
  · EKF
end note
@enduml

Quaternion avoids gimbal lock inherent to Euler angles.

docs.title: Subsystem — Altimeter MS5607
docs.text: Sensor: MS5607-02BA03-50 — TE Connectivity Measures: Pressure (10–1200 hPa) · Temperature (−40…+85 °C) · Altitude (m) Hardware target: SPI on GPIO8–11 Current firmware: I2C via bundled MS5607 library (Libnexamples/MS5607-master/) Files: include/altimeter.h · src/altimeter.cpp

docs.title: 1. Hardware Connections
docs.text:
@startuml
skinparam componentStyle rectangle

node "Raspberry Pi Pico (RP2040)" {
    component "GPIO8  (SPI MISO / SDO)" as MISO
    component "GPIO9  (SPI CS  / CSB)"  as CS
    component "GPIO10 (SPI SCK / SCLK)" as SCK
    component "GPIO11 (SPI MOSI / SDI)" as MOSI
}

component "MS5607\n(pressure + temp)" as MS {
    component "ADC\n24-bit" as ADC_MS
    component "PROM\n(calibration\ncoefficients C1-C6)" as PROM
}

MISO <-- MS  : SDO (sensor → MCU)
CS   --> MS  : CSB (active LOW)
SCK  --> MS  : SCLK
MOSI --> MS  : SDI (MCU → sensor)

note bottom of MS
  Current firmware uses I2C (library limitation).
  SPI pins reserved on PCB.
  Replace _sensor driver in altimeter.h/.cpp
  when an SPI MS5607 library is available.
end note
@enduml
GPIO SPI signal MS5607 pin Status GPIO8 MISO SDO Reserved — not driven by current firmware GPIO9 CS CSB Reserved — not driven by current firmware GPIO10 SCK SCLK Reserved — not driven by current firmware GPIO11 MOSI SDI Reserved — not driven by current firmware

docs.title: 2. Class Diagram
docs.text:
@startuml
skinparam classAttributeIconSize 0

abstract class SensorModule

class AltimeterModule {
    - _sensor    : MS5607
    - _ready     : bool
    - _failCount : uint8_t
    - _error     : SensorError
    --
    + init()      : bool
    + read()      : bool
    + isReady()   : bool
    + name()      : const char*
    + lastError() : SensorError
}

class MS5607 {
    + begin()             : bool
    + readDigitalValue()  : bool
    + getTemperature()    : float
    + getPressure()       : float
    + getAltitude()       : float
}

note right of MS5607
  Bundled library — I2C implementation.
  Located in Libnexamples/MS5607-master/
  Uses Wire internally.
end note

SensorModule    <|--  AltimeterModule
AltimeterModule  *--  MS5607 : "_sensor"
@enduml

docs.title: 3. Initialization Sequence — init()
docs.text:
@startuml
participant AltimeterModule as alt
participant MS5607          as lib

alt -> lib : _sensor.begin()
note right : reads PROM calibration\ncoefficients C1-C6 via I2C

alt lib found
    lib --> alt : true
    alt -> alt  : _ready = true\n_error = OK
    alt -> alt  : Serial.println("[Altimeter] OK")
else not found
    lib --> alt : false
    alt -> alt  : _error = NOT_FOUND\n_ready = false
    alt -> alt  : Serial.println("[Altimeter] not found")
end
@enduml

docs.title: 4. Measurement Sequence — read()
docs.text:
@startuml
participant AltimeterModule  as alt
participant MS5607            as lib
participant "g_adcs.altimeter" as data

alt -> lib : _sensor.readDigitalValue()
note right of lib
  I2C transactions:
  · trigger D1 (pressure ADC)  → wait
  · read D1 (3 bytes)
  · trigger D2 (temp ADC)      → wait
  · read D2 (3 bytes)
  · 2nd-order compensation
  NOP library ≈ 36 ops
end note

alt lib OK
    lib --> alt : true
    alt -> lib  : getTemperature() → T
    alt -> lib  : getPressure()    → P
    alt -> lib  : getAltitude()    → alt_m
    note right : getAltitude uses pow()\nNOP ≈ 23 ops

    alt T and P in valid range
        alt -> data : pressure    = P    [hPa]
        alt -> data : temperature = T    [°C]
        alt -> data : altitude    = alt_m [m]
        alt -> data : valid = true
        alt -> alt  : _failCount = 0\n_error = OK
        alt --> alt : return true
    else out of range
        alt -> data : valid = false
        alt -> alt  : _error = DATA_INVALID
        alt --> alt : return false
    end

else read failed
    lib --> alt  : false
    alt  -> data : valid = false
    alt  -> alt  : _error = READ_FAIL\n_failCount++
    alt  --> alt : return false
end
@enduml

docs.title: 5. MS5607 Compensation Formulas
docs.text:
@startuml
start

:Read raw values D1 (pressure), D2 (temperature)
 from ADC via I2C;

:Read PROM coefficients C1…C6
 (done once in begin());

partition "Temperature compensation" {
    :dT   = D2 − C5 × 2^8;
    :TEMP = 2000 + dT × C6 / 2^23;
}

partition "Pressure compensation" {
    :OFF  = C2 × 2^17 + (C4 × dT) / 2^6;
    :SENS = C1 × 2^16 + (C3 × dT) / 2^7;
    :P    = (D1 × SENS / 2^21 − OFF) / 2^15;
}

if (TEMP < 2000  [T < 20 °C]?) then (yes)
    partition "2nd-order correction" {
        :T2    = dT^2 / 2^31;
        :OFF2  = 61 × (TEMP−2000)^2 / 2^4;
        :SENS2 = 2  × (TEMP−2000)^2;
        :apply corrections to TEMP, OFF, SENS, P;
    }
endif

:getAltitude():
 alt = 44330 × (1 − (P/101325)^0.190295);

stop
@enduml

docs.title: 6. Validation Ranges (firmware-enforced)
docs.text:
@startuml
start

:P = getPressure()
T = getTemperature();

if (P < 10 or P > 1200 hPa\nor T < -40 or T > 85 °C?) then (yes)
    :_error = DATA_INVALID
valid = false;
    stop
else (no)
    :write P, T, alt to g_adcs
valid = true;
    stop
endif
@enduml
Field Valid range Pressure 10 – 1200 hPa Temperature −40 °C – +85 °C Altitude derived — not independently validated

docs.title: 7. Output — g_adcs.altimeter
docs.text: Field Unit Source pressure hPa MS5607 ADC + compensation temperature °C MS5607 ADC + compensation altitude m ISA barometric formula valid bool false on read fail or out-of-range

docs.title: Subsystem — External Watchdog
docs.text: Role: External watchdog module — monitors ADCS health, resets RP2040 via RUN pin on timeout Interface: I2C0 (GPIO4/5) + CLK output (GPIO19) I2C address: 0x55 (watchdog side — confirm before deployment) Files: include/watchdog.h · src/watchdog.cpp

docs.title: 1. Hardware Connections
docs.text:
@startuml
skinparam componentStyle rectangle

node "Raspberry Pi Pico (RP2040)" {
    component "GPIO4  (I2C0 SDA)" as SDA
    component "GPIO5  (I2C0 SCL)" as SCL
    component "GPIO19 (CLK OUT)"  as CLK
    component "RUN    (reset IN)" as RUN
}

component "External Watchdog\nModule (0x55)" as WD {
    component "I2C Slave\n(receives status)" as I2C_S
    component "CLK counter\n(timeout detect)"  as CTR
    component "Reset driver\n(open-drain)"      as RST
}

SDA <--> I2C_S : SDA (I2C0)
SCL  --> I2C_S : SCL (I2C0)
CLK  --> CTR   : CLK pulse (1 kHz)\nidles LOW
RST  --> RUN   : RESET (active LOW)

note bottom of RST
  If CLK stops or status byte
  indicates sensor failure,
  watchdog pulls RUN LOW
  to reset the RP2040.
end note
@enduml
GPIO Role Direction State GPIO4 I2C0 SDA bidirectional managed by Wire (default Mbed Pico pins) GPIO5 I2C0 SCL output managed by Wire GPIO19 CLK output OUTPUT idles LOW; pulsed once per read() RUN hardware reset INPUT pulled LOW by watchdog on timeout

docs.title: 2. Class Diagram
docs.text:
@startuml
skinparam classAttributeIconSize 0

abstract class SensorModule

class WatchdogComm {
    - _ready : bool
    - _error : SensorError
    --
    + init()              : bool
    + read()              : bool
    + isReady()           : bool
    + name()              : const char*
    + lastError()         : SensorError
    --
    + sendStatus(byte: uint8_t)
    - tickClock()
}

note right of WatchdogComm
  Constants (watchdog.h):
  WATCHDOG_I2C_ADDR = 0x55
  WD_CLK_HALF_US    = 500 µs
  ──────────────────────────
  Status byte bit-field:
  bit 0 WD_STATUS_IMU_OK
  bit 1 WD_STATUS_ALT_OK
  bit 2 WD_STATUS_PT_OK
  bits 3-7 reserved
end note

class Wire <<library>> {
    + begin()
    + beginTransmission(addr)
    + write(byte)
    + endTransmission() : uint8_t
}

SensorModule  <|--  WatchdogComm
WatchdogComm  ..>   Wire : uses (I2C0 GPIO4/5)
@enduml

docs.title: 3. Initialization Sequence — init()
docs.text:
@startuml
participant WatchdogComm as wd
participant "Arduino\nGPIO"  as gpio
participant "Wire\n(I2C0)"   as wire

wd   -> gpio : pinMode(GPIO19, OUTPUT)
wd   -> gpio : digitalWrite(GPIO19, LOW)  ' CLK idles LOW
wd   -> wire : Wire.begin()
note right : I2C0 on GPIO4/5\n(Mbed Pico default — no pin remapping needed)

wd   -> wire : Wire.beginTransmission(0x55)
wd   -> wire : Wire.endTransmission() → err

alt err == 0  [watchdog responds]
    wd -> wd   : _ready = true\n_error = OK
    wd -> wd   : g_adcs.watchdog.connected = true
    wd -> wd   : Serial.println("[Watchdog] OK")
else err != 0  [NACK — absent or powered off]
    wd -> wd   : _error = NOT_FOUND\n_ready = false
    wd -> wd   : g_adcs.watchdog.connected = false
    wd -> wd   : Serial.println("[Watchdog] not found")
    note right : Non-fatal — other modules\ncontinue normally
end
@enduml

docs.title: 4. Per-cycle Sequence — read() + sendStatus()
docs.text:
@startuml
participant "main.cpp"       as main
participant WatchdogComm     as wd
participant "Wire (I2C0)"    as wire
participant "GPIO19 (CLK)"   as clk
participant "g_adcs.watchdog" as data

main -> wd   : read()

wd   -> wd   : tickClock()
wd   -> clk  : digitalWrite(HIGH)
wd   -> wd   : delayMicroseconds(500)
wd   -> clk  : digitalWrite(LOW)
wd   -> wd   : delayMicroseconds(500)
note right : 1 full CLK pulse\nperiod = 1000 µs = 1 kHz

wd   -> wire : Wire.beginTransmission(0x55)
wd   -> wire : Wire.endTransmission() → err

alt err == 0
    wd   -> data : connected = true
    wd   --> wd  : return true
else err != 0
    wd   -> data : connected = false
    wd   -> wd   : _error = NOT_FOUND
    wd   --> wd  : return false
end

main -> wd   : sendStatus(status_byte)
wd   -> wire : Wire.beginTransmission(0x55)
wd   -> wire : Wire.write(status_byte)
wd   -> wire : Wire.endTransmission()
@enduml

docs.title: 5. Status Byte — sendStatus()
docs.text:
@startuml
start

:build status_byte = 0;

if (g_adcs.imu.valid?) then (yes)
    :status_byte |= 0x01  [bit 0 IMU_OK];
endif

if (g_adcs.altimeter.valid?) then (yes)
    :status_byte |= 0x02  [bit 1 ALT_OK];
endif

if (g_adcs.pt1000.active_count > 0?) then (yes)
    :status_byte |= 0x04  [bit 2 PT_OK];
endif

:WatchdogComm::sendStatus(status_byte)
I2C write → 0x55;

stop
@enduml
Bit Mask Meaning 0 0x01 (WD_STATUS_IMU_OK) g_adcs.imu.valid == true 1 0x02 (WD_STATUS_ALT_OK) g_adcs.altimeter.valid == true 2 0x04 (WD_STATUS_PT_OK) g_adcs.pt1000.active_count > 0 3–7 — reserved

docs.title: 6. CLK Signal Timing
docs.text:
@startuml
title GPIO19 CLK — WD_CLK_HALF_US = 500 µs · period = 1 ms · one pulse per loop() cycle (1 s)

robust "GPIO19 CLK" as CLK

@0
CLK is LOW

@1000
CLK is HIGH

@1500
CLK is LOW

@2500
CLK is HIGH

@3000
CLK is LOW

@3000
note top of CLK : one pulse = tickClock()
@enduml

The CLK pin idles LOW. One pulse is sent at the start of each read() call. The watchdog uses the absence of this pulse (over its timeout period) to trigger a reset.

docs.title: GNSS M9N — RP2040 Architecture
docs.text: Module: u-blox M9N GNSS receiver interfaced to a Raspberry Pi Pico (RP2040) Framework: Arduino (PlatformIO, Mbed variant) Last Updated: May 29, 2026

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text:
The M9N is a concurrent GNSS receiver (GPS / GLONASS / Galileo / BeiDou). It exposes two independent communication ports used simultaneously:

Port Direction Purpose I2C (400 kHz) M9N → RP2040 Stream NMEA sentences (GGA, RMC, GSA) UART (38400 baud) RP2040 → M9N Send UBX configuration commands

NMEA data flows in on I2C; configuration commands go out on UART. The two ports operate independently — configuring via UART does not disturb the NMEA stream on I2C.

docs.title: Wiring Diagram
docs.text:
RP2040                          u-blox M9N
──────                          ──────────
3.3 V ──────────────────────── VCC
GND   ──────────────────────── GND

GPIO4 (SDA) ←──── 4k7 ── 3V3   SDA    (I2C0, 400 kHz)
GPIO5 (SCL) ──── 4k7 ─── 3V3   SCL

GPIO16 (TX1) ──────────────── UART_RX  (UBX commands, 38400 baud)
GPIO17 (RX1) ←─────────────── UART_TX  (reserved)

I2C address: 0x42 (M9N default, not configurable in hardware).

docs.title: 3.1 I2C — Reading NMEA Data
docs.text:
The M9N maintains an internal byte-FIFO. Registers 0xFD (MSB) and 0xFE (LSB) expose the number of bytes waiting. Unused positions in the FIFO are filled with 0xFF padding.

Read sequence (executed each update() call):

1. Write register pointer 0xFD to M9N (repeated-start)
2. Read 2 bytes → available = (byte[0] << 8) | byte[1]
3. While available > 0:
     chunk = min(available, 255)
     requestFrom(0x42, chunk)
     feed each byte to NMEA state machine
     available -= chunk

docs.title: 3.2 UART — Sending UBX Configuration
docs.text:
UBX is u-blox's binary protocol for configuration. begin() sends one UBX-CFG-VALSET frame to set the dynamic model to AIRBORNE_2G (UAV / drone profile, up to 2g acceleration).

UBX-CFG-VALSET frame (17 bytes):

Offset  Bytes  Value         Description
──────  ─────  ──────────    ───────────────────────────────────
0       2      B5 62         Sync chars
2       2      06 8A         Class / ID = UBX-CFG-VALSET
4       2      09 00         Payload length = 9
6       1      00            Version
7       1      07            Layers: RAM (b0) | BBR (b1) | Flash (b2)
8       2      00 00         Reserved
10      4      21 00 11 20   Key = CFG-NAVSPG-DYNMODEL (0x20110021, LE)
14      1      <model>       Dynamic model value (0x07 = AIRBORNE_2G)
15      2      CK_A CK_B     Fletcher-8 checksum over bytes [2..14]

Available dynamic models (defined in gnss.h):

Constant Value Use case UBX_DYNMODEL_PORTABLE 0 Default UBX_DYNMODEL_STATIONARY 2 Fixed installation UBX_DYNMODEL_AIRBORNE_1G 6 High-altitude balloon UBX_DYNMODEL_AIRBORNE_2G 7 UAV / drone ← active UBX_DYNMODEL_AIRBORNE_4G 8 Rocket / aggressive flight

docs.title: 4.1 Files
docs.text:
include/gnss.h      — GPSData struct, GNSS class declaration, UBX model constants
src/gnss.cpp        — GNSS class implementation + NMEA parsers (file-scope static)
include/config.h    — Pin assignments and bus parameters

docs.title: 4.2 GPSData Struct
docs.text:
struct GPSData {
  double   latitude;    // decimal degrees, + N / − S
  double   longitude;   // decimal degrees, + E / − W
  double   altitudeM;   // metres above MSL

  uint8_t  fixQuality;  // GGA field 6: 0=none, 1=GPS, 2=DGPS …
  uint8_t  fixType;     // GSA field 2: 1=none, 2=2D, 3=3D
  uint8_t  satellites;  // satellites in use

  double   speedKnots;
  double   courseDeg;

  uint8_t  hour, minute, second;   // UTC time from GGA/RMC
  uint8_t  day, month;
  uint16_t year;                   // from RMC

  bool     valid;   // true when fixQuality > 0 (GGA) or status == 'A' (RMC)
};

docs.title: 4.3 GNSS Class Interface
docs.text:
class GNSS {
public:
  bool            begin();        // I2C check, UART init, set AIRBORNE_4G model
  void            update();       // drain M9N I2C FIFO, parse NMEA
  const GPSData&  getData() const;
  bool            hasNewFix() const;  // true for one update() cycle after new fix
};

docs.title: 4.4 NMEA Parser — Static Scope
docs.text:
All parsing helpers are file-scope static (not class members). This keeps the class interface clean while allowing the parsers to write directly into the active GPSData instance via module-level pointers set in begin().

feedByte(b)
  │
  ├─ 0xFF → discard (M9N padding)
  ├─ '$'  → start new sentence buffer
  ├─ CR/LF → sentence complete → dispatchNMEA()
  └─ else → append to nmeaBuf[128]

dispatchNMEA(sentence)
  │
  ├─ nmeaChecksum() → drop if bad XOR
  ├─ "**GGA" → parseGGA()  — position, time, fix quality, altitude
  ├─ "**RMC" → parseRMC()  — position, time, speed, course, date, validity
  └─ "**GSA" → parseGSA()  — fix type (1/2/3)

Talker-agnostic matching: the parser skips the 2-char talker prefix (GP, GN, GL, GA) and matches only on the 3-char sentence type (GGA, RMC, GSA).

docs.title: 4.5 Data Flow
docs.text:
M9N FIFO (I2C registers 0xFD/0xFE)
        │
        ▼  up to 255 bytes per chunk
   feedByte() × N
        │
        ▼  on CR/LF
   dispatchNMEA()
        │
   ┌────┴──────────────┐
   ▼                   ▼
parseGGA()         parseRMC()      parseGSA()
  ↓ lat/lon/alt      ↓ speed/date    ↓ fixType
  ↓ fixQuality       ↓ valid flag
        └──────────────┴──────────────┘
                       ▼
                  GPSData struct
                       ▼
               getData() / hasNewFix()

docs.title: 5. Configuration (include/config.h)
docs.text:
// I2C0 — GNSS NMEA data
#define I2C_SDA_PIN       4
#define I2C_SCL_PIN       5
#define I2C_CLOCK_HZ      400000
#define GNSS_I2C_ADDR     0x42

// UART1 — UBX config commands
#define GNSS_UART_TX_PIN  16
#define GNSS_UART_RX_PIN  17
#define GNSS_UART_BAUD    38400

docs.title: 6. Build System
docs.text:
[env:pico]
platform        = raspberrypi
board           = pico
framework       = arduino
upload_protocol = picotool

No external GNSS library is used. The NMEA parser and UBX framing are implemented directly in gnss.cpp.

pio run                    # compile
pio run --target upload    # compile + upload (BOOTSEL mode)
pio device monitor -b 115200

docs.title: 7. Typical Usage in main.cpp
docs.text:
#include "gnss.h"
#include "config.h"
#include <Wire.h>

GNSS gnss;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  Wire.setSDA(I2C_SDA_PIN);
  Wire.setSCL(I2C_SCL_PIN);
  Wire.begin();
  Wire.setClock(I2C_CLOCK_HZ);

  if (!gnss.begin()) {
    Serial.println("M9N not found on I2C");
    while (true);
  }
}

void loop() {
  gnss.update();
  if (gnss.hasNewFix()) {
    const GPSData& d = gnss.getData();
    Serial.printf("%.6f, %.6f  alt=%.1fm  sats=%d\n",
                  d.latitude, d.longitude, d.altitudeM, d.satellites);
  }
  delay(10);
}

docs.title: 8. Known Constraints
docs.text:
• I2C bus shared: Wire (I2C0) is shared with anything else on GPIO4/5. The M9N does not support clock-stretching beyond what the RP2040 I2C peripheral tolerates; keep other devices off this bus or schedule reads carefully.
  • I2C blocking: A full FIFO drain (~1 KB at 400 kHz) takes ~25 ms.
  • UBX ACK not checked: sendUBX() writes to UART and returns immediately. The M9N will ACK or NAK on UART_TX but GPIO17 RX is not read — add a response parser if config confirmation is needed.
  • 0xFF padding: The M9N pads its I2C output buffer with 0xFF when idle. feedByte() silently discards these; they are not NMEA data.

docs.title: SAFARI ADCS — PlantUML Diagrams & Explanations
docs.text: This document provides visual representations of the SAFARI ADCS firmware architecture using PlantUML.

docs.title: 1. System Architecture Overview
docs.text:
Shows all major modules and their interconnections within the RP2040.

@startuml SAFARI_ADCS_Architecture
!define RECTANGLE_COLOR #E1F5FE
!define PROCESS_COLOR #B3E5FC
!define EXTERNAL_COLOR #FFF9C4

skinparam rectangle {
  BackgroundColor RECTANGLE_COLOR
  BorderColor #01579B
  FontColor #01579B
}

package "RP2040 Microcontroller" {
  rectangle "Main Orchestrator\n(main.cpp)" as main {
  }

  package "Communication Interfaces" {
    rectangle "I2C Bus\n(GPIO 4, 5 @ 400kHz)" as i2c
    rectangle "UART0 Serial\n(GPIO 0, 1 @ 115200)" as serial
  }

  package "Hardware Modules" {
    rectangle "GNSS Module\n(gnss.h/cpp)" as gnss
    rectangle "Sensor Manager\n(sensors.h/cpp)" as sensorMgr
    rectangle "Watchdog Module\n(watchdog.h/cpp)" as watchdog
    rectangle "Status Display\n(printStatus.h/cpp)" as display
  }

  package "Concrete Sensors" {
    rectangle "Pt1000\nThermometer" as pt1000
    rectangle "IMU\n(6-axis)" as imu
    rectangle "Altimeter\n(Barometric)" as altimeter
    rectangle "Sensor Slot 4\n(Reserved)" as slot4
  }

  package "Configuration" {
    rectangle "config.h\n(Pins, Addresses, Constants)" as config
  }
}

package "External Hardware" {
  rectangle "M9N u-blox\nGNSS Receiver\n(I2C 0x42)" as m9n
  rectangle "MPU-6050 or\nICM-42688P\n(I2C 0x68)" as imuHW
  rectangle "BMP280 or\nMS5611\n(I2C 0x77)" as altimeterHW
  rectangle "PT1000 RTD\nwith MAX31865\n(ADC/SPI)" as pt1000HW
  rectangle "External Watchdog\nController" as watchdogHW
}

main --> gnss
main --> sensorMgr
main --> watchdog
main --> display

gnss --> i2c
gnss --> m9n

sensorMgr --> pt1000
sensorMgr --> imu
sensorMgr --> altimeter
sensorMgr --> slot4

pt1000 --> i2c
imu --> i2c
altimeter --> i2c

pt1000 --> pt1000HW
imu --> imuHW
altimeter --> altimeterHW

i2c --> m9n
i2c --> imuHW
i2c --> altimeterHW

display --> serial
watchdog --> watchdogHW

config -.-> main
config -.-> gnss
config -.-> sensorMgr

@enduml

docs.title: Explanation:
docs.text:
• Main Orchestrator: Central loop (main.cpp) controls all subsystems
  • Communication Buses: I2C for all sensors (400 kHz), UART for debug/status
  • Modules: GNSS, Sensor Manager, Watchdog, and Status Display operate independently but coordinated
  • Hardware Devices: Each module interfaces with physical devices via I2C/ADC
  • Configuration: Central config.h provides all tunable parameters

docs.title: 2. Sensor Framework Class Hierarchy
docs.text:
Demonstrates the polymorphic sensor architecture.

@startuml Sensor_ClassDiagram
skinparam classBackgroundColor #E8F5E9
skinparam classArrowColor #2E7D32
skinparam classBorderColor #2E7D32

abstract class Sensor {
  {abstract} +begin(): bool
  {abstract} +update(): void
  {abstract} +getData(): SensorData
}

class SensorData {
  +value: float
  +valid: bool
  +timestamp: uint32_t
}

struct Pt1000Data {
  +tempC: float
  +resistanceOhm: float
}

struct IMUData {
  +accelX: float
  +accelY: float
  +accelZ: float
  +gyroX: float
  +gyroY: float
  +gyroZ: float
  +tempC: float
}

struct AltimeterData {
  +pressureHPa: float
  +altitudeM: float
  +tempC: float
}

class Pt1000Sensor {
  -data: Pt1000Data
  +begin(): bool
  +update(): void
  +getData(): SensorData
  +getPt1000(): const Pt1000Data&
}

class IMUSensor {
  -data: IMUData
  +begin(): bool
  +update(): void
  +getData(): SensorData
  +getIMU(): const IMUData&
}

class AltimeterSensor {
  -data: AltimeterData
  -seaLevelPressure: float
  +begin(): bool
  +update(): void
  +getData(): SensorData
  +getAltimeter(): const AltimeterData&
}

class SensorManager {
  -sensors[4]: Sensor*
  -count: uint8_t
  +addSensor(sensor: Sensor*): bool
  +updateAll(): void
  +getSensorData(idx: uint8_t): SensorData
  +getSensor(idx: uint8_t): Sensor*
  +getCount(): uint8_t
}

Sensor <|-- Pt1000Sensor
Sensor <|-- IMUSensor
Sensor <|-- AltimeterSensor

Pt1000Sensor --> Pt1000Data
IMUSensor --> IMUData
AltimeterSensor --> AltimeterData

Sensor --> SensorData
SensorManager --> "1..4" Sensor
SensorManager --> SensorData

@enduml

docs.title: Explanation:
docs.text:
• Sensor (base class): Defines interface that all sensors must implement
  • Concrete Sensors: Pt1000Sensor, IMUSensor, AltimeterSensor each inherit from Sensor
  • Type-specific Data: Each sensor has its own data struct (Pt1000Data, IMUData, AltimeterData)
  • Generic Adapter: Each concrete class implements getData() returning SensorData for uniform display
  • SensorManager: Manages up to 4 sensor instances, can call updateAll() to refresh all simultaneously

docs.title: 3. GNSS Module Data Flow
docs.text:
Details the GNSS receiver communication and NMEA parsing pipeline.

@startuml GNSS_DataFlow
skinparam rectangle {
  BackgroundColor #FFF3E0
  BorderColor #E65100
}

rectangle "GNSS Module (gnss.cpp)" {

  rectangle "I2C Protocol Layer" {
    rectangle "Read Register 0xFD\n(Byte Count)" as reg_fd
    rectangle "Read Register 0xFE\n(Byte Count Hi)" as reg_fe
    rectangle "Read I2C Data\n(up to 255 bytes)" as read_data
  }

  rectangle "NMEA Processing" {
    rectangle "Feed bytes to\nState Machine" as state_machine
    rectangle "Identify Sentence\nType" as identify
    rectangle "Parse NMEA\nSentence" as parse
  }

  rectangle "Sentence Parsers" {
    rectangle "parseGGA()\nPosition, Fix, Sats" as parser_gga
    rectangle "parseRMC()\nDate, Speed, Course" as parser_rmc
    rectangle "parseGSA()\nFix Type (2D/3D)" as parser_gsa
  }

  rectangle "Data Storage" {
    rectangle "Update GPSData\nStruct" as update_struct
  }
}

rectangle "M9N Receiver\n(External Hardware)" {
  rectangle "NMEA Sentence\nBuffer" as buffer
}

buffer --> reg_fd
buffer --> reg_fe
reg_fd --> read_data
reg_fe --> read_data

read_data --> state_machine
state_machine --> identify

identify --> parser_gga
identify --> parser_rmc
identify --> parser_gsa

parser_gga --> parse
parser_rmc --> parse
parser_gsa --> parse

parse --> update_struct

update_struct --> "gnss.getData()"

note right of reg_fd
  Polls byte count
  from receiver
end note

note right of state_machine
  0xFF padding bytes
  skipped automatically
end note

note right of parser_gga
  Extracts: Lat, Lon, Alt
  Fix quality, Satellite count
end note

@enduml

docs.title: Explanation:
docs.text:
• I2C Protocol: Queries M9N byte count registers, reads up to 255 bytes per cycle
  • NMEA State Machine: Processes incoming bytes, identifies sentence boundaries
  • Sentence Identification: GGA (position/fix), RMC (date/speed), GSA (fix type)
  • Parsers: Each parser extracts relevant fields using field delimiters and coordinates conversion
  • Data Storage: Parsed values update the shared GPSData struct accessible to main loop

docs.title: 4. Main Loop Execution Flow
docs.text:
Sequence diagram showing one iteration of the main control loop.

@startuml Main_Loop_Sequence
participant "main.cpp\nLoop" as loop
participant "GNSS Module" as gnss
participant "Sensor Manager" as sensors
participant "Display Module" as display
participant "Watchdog Module" as watchdog

loop Every ~50ms

  loop Continuous Update
    loop --> gnss: update()
    gnss --> loop: (~10ms)

    loop --> sensors: updateAll()
    sensors --> loop: (< 5ms)
  end

  alt Every 1 second (1Hz)
    loop --> display: printStatus()
    display --> loop: (~20ms)
    note right of display
      Builds 4-column table,
      sends to Serial
    end note
  end

  alt Every 5 seconds
    loop --> watchdog: sendHeartbeat()
    watchdog --> loop: (<1ms)
    note right of watchdog
      Sends "alive" signal
      to external watchdog
    end note
  end

  loop --> loop: delay(10ms)\n(yield to OS)

end

note over loop, watchdog
  Nominal cycle: ~50ms
  Margin: ~15ms for anomalies
  Total time budget: 50ms
end note

@enduml

docs.title: Explanation:
docs.text:
• Continuous Update: GNSS and Sensor updates run every cycle (~10 ms for GNSS, <5 ms for sensors)
  • 1 Hz Display: Status printed to serial every second without blocking
  • 5 Hz Watchdog: Heartbeat sent every 5 seconds to external system
  • Timing Budget: 50 ms cycle with 15 ms margin maintains real-time responsiveness

docs.title: 5. I2C Bus Topology
docs.text:
Physical layout of all I2C devices on the RP2040.

@startuml I2C_Topology
skinparam rectangle {
  BackgroundColor #E0E0E0
  BorderColor #424242
}

rectangle "RP2040 I2C Bus" {
  rectangle "GPIO 4 (SDA)\nGPIO 5 (SCL)\n400 kHz" as bus_control
}

rectangle "Connected Devices" {
  rectangle "M9N GNSS\nI2C Address: 0x42\n(UART protocol via I2C)" as m9n_dev
  rectangle "IMU\nI2C Address: 0x68\n(MPU-6050, ICM-42688P, LSM6DS3)" as imu_dev
  rectangle "Altimeter\nI2C Address: 0x77\n(BMP280, MS5611, LPS22HB)" as alt_dev
}

rectangle "Other Interfaces" {
  rectangle "PT1000 RTD\nADC or SPI\n(MAX31865)" as pt1000_dev
}

bus_control --> m9n_dev
bus_control --> imu_dev
bus_control --> alt_dev

note right of m9n_dev
  Pull-up resistors on
  SDA/SCL required
end note

note right of imu_dev
  Address configurable
  via AD0 pin
end note

note right of alt_dev
  Address configurable
  via SDO pin
end note

note right of pt1000_dev
  Separate from I2C
  Uses ADC or dedicated SPI
end note

@enduml

docs.title: Explanation:
docs.text:
• I2C Master: RP2040 acts as master on GPIO 4 (SDA) and GPIO 5 (SCL)
  • Bus Speed: 400 kHz suitable for all connected devices
  • Device Addresses: M9N at 0x42, IMU at 0x68, Altimeter at 0x77
  • PT1000: Uses separate ADC or SPI interface (not on I2C bus)
  • Extensibility: Address lines can be configured to support multiple instances

docs.title: 6. Status Display Layout
docs.text:
Visual representation of the 4-column status output to serial console.

@startuml Status_Display_Layout
skinparam rectangle {
  BackgroundColor #FFF8E1
  BorderColor #FF6F00
}

rectangle "Serial Console Output (115200 baud)" {

  rectangle "Column 1: GPS Data\n(11 lines)" as col1 {
    rectangle "Fix quality" as gps1
    rectangle "Fix type" as gps2
    rectangle "Satellites" as gps3
    rectangle "Valid" as gps4
    rectangle "Date (UTC)" as gps5
    rectangle "Time (UTC)" as gps6
    rectangle "Latitude" as gps7
    rectangle "Longitude" as gps8
    rectangle "Altitude (MSL)" as gps9
    rectangle "Speed" as gps10
    rectangle "Course" as gps11
  }

  rectangle "Column 2: System Status\n(6 lines)" as col2 {
    rectangle "GNSS Fix" as sys1
    rectangle "Satellites" as sys2
    rectangle "Sensors Active" as sys3
    rectangle "Watchdog Status" as sys4
    rectangle "[Spare]" as sys5
    rectangle "[Spare]" as sys6
  }

  rectangle "Column 3: Sensor 1 (Pt1000)\n(5 lines)" as col3 {
    rectangle "Name" as sen1_1
    rectangle "Temperature" as sen1_2
    rectangle "Resistance" as sen1_3
    rectangle "Status" as sen1_4
    rectangle "[Spare]" as sen1_5
  }

  rectangle "Column 4: Sensor 2+ (IMU, Altimeter)\n(5 lines)" as col4 {
    rectangle "Name" as sen2_1
    rectangle "Primary Value" as sen2_2
    rectangle "Secondary Value" as sen2_3
    rectangle "Status" as sen2_4
    rectangle "[Spare]" as sen2_5
  }
}

note bottom of col1, col4
  Each column ~36 characters wide
  Refresh rate: 1 second (1 Hz)
  Uses box-drawing characters (│─┌┐└┘)
end note

@enduml

docs.title: Explanation:
docs.text:
• Column 1 (GPS): 11 lines of position, time, velocity data from GNSS
  • Column 2 (System): 6 lines showing overall system health and sensor count
  • Columns 3-4 (Sensors): Up to 4 sensor columns, each 5 lines with sensor-specific data
  • Alignment: All columns printed horizontally with box-drawing borders
  • Refresh: Updates every 1 second without blocking main loop

docs.title: 7. Sensor Update Sequence
docs.text:
Detailed timeline of how each sensor is queried and updated.

@startuml Sensor_Update_Sequence
participant "Main Loop" as main
participant "SensorMgr" as mgr
participant "Pt1000Sensor" as pt1000
participant "IMUSensor" as imu
participant "AltimeterSensor" as alt

main --> mgr: updateAll()

mgr --> pt1000: update()
activate pt1000
pt1000 --> pt1000: Read ADC/SPI
pt1000 --> pt1000: Convert to Temperature
pt1000 --> pt1000: Store in Pt1000Data
deactivate pt1000

mgr --> imu: update()
activate imu
imu --> imu: Read I2C (0x68)
imu --> imu: Parse accel/gyro/temp
imu --> imu: Store in IMUData
deactivate imu

mgr --> alt: update()
activate alt
alt --> alt: Read I2C (0x77)
alt --> alt: Parse pressure
alt --> alt: Convert to altitude
alt --> alt: Store in AltimeterData
deactivate alt

mgr --> main: return

note right of mgr
  Total time: < 5ms
  (stub implementations)
end note

@enduml

docs.title: Explanation:
docs.text:
• Sequential Update: SensorManager calls update() on each sensor in order
  • Pt1000: Reads ADC/SPI and converts resistance to temperature
  • IMU: Queries I2C @ 0x68 for 6-axis accel/gyro + die temperature
  • Altimeter: Reads pressure from I2C @ 0x77 and calculates barometric altitude
  • Timing: All sensors update in <5 ms per cycle

docs.title: 8. Watchdog Communication Protocol
docs.text:
Interaction between RP2040 and external watchdog controller.

@startuml Watchdog_Protocol
participant "Main Loop\n(RP2040)" as rp2040
participant "External Watchdog\nController" as watchdog

rp2040 --> watchdog: sendHeartbeat()\nevery 5 seconds

activate watchdog
watchdog --> watchdog: Update last_heartbeat timestamp
watchdog --> watchdog: Check timeout threshold
watchdog --> watchdog: Update WatchdogStatus
deactivate watchdog

alt Normal Operation
  watchdog --> rp2040: OK (healthy)
else Timeout Detected
  watchdog --> rp2040: ALERT (RP2040 hung)
  watchdog --> watchdog: Trigger watchdog action\n(reset, power cycle, etc.)
end

rp2040 --> watchdog: sendAlert(code)\nif emergency detected

activate watchdog
watchdog --> watchdog: Log alert code
watchdog --> watchdog: Take emergency action
deactivate watchdog

rp2040 --> watchdog: isWatchdogAlive()\nquery status

activate watchdog
watchdog --> rp2040: return WatchdogStatus
deactivate watchdog

note right of rp2040
  Heartbeat interval: 5 seconds
  Watchdog timeout: ~10-15 seconds (TBD)
  Protocol: TBD (UART, I2C, CAN)
end note

@enduml

docs.title: Explanation:
docs.text:
• Heartbeat (5 sec): RP2040 sends periodic "alive" signal to watchdog
  • Timeout Detection: Watchdog tracks last heartbeat; triggers if not received within threshold
  • Emergency Alerts: RP2040 can send alert codes for critical conditions
  • Status Query: RP2040 can query watchdog health to ensure external system is responsive
  • Protocol TBD: Communication method (UART, I2C, or CAN) to be defined in future

docs.title: 9. Initialization Flow (setup())
docs.text:
Startup sequence that initializes all subsystems.

@startuml Initialization_Flow
start

:Initialize Serial @ 115200;
:Initialize Wire (I2C);
:Wire.setClock(400kHz);

:gnss.begin();
if (M9N responds?) then (no)
  :Print error, halt;
  stop
else (yes)
endif

:sensorMgr.addSensor(new Pt1000Sensor());
:sensorMgr.addSensor(new IMUSensor());
:sensorMgr.addSensor(new AltimeterSensor());

note right
  Sensors call begin()
  in addSensor()
end note

:watchdog.begin();

:Print boot banner;
:Wait for GNSS first fix;

:Enter main loop;
stop

@enduml

docs.title: Explanation:
docs.text:
• Serial Init: Debug/status output at 115200 baud
  • I2C Init: Wire at 400 kHz on GPIO 4/5
  • GNSS Check: Must detect M9N at 0x42, otherwise halt
  • Sensor Registration: Sensors added to manager in order (calls their begin())
  • Watchdog Init: External system interface initialized
  • Boot Banner: User notification that system is alive
  • First Fix Wait: Wait for initial GNSS position lock before entering main loop

docs.title: 10. Error Handling & Recovery
docs.text:
State diagram showing system responses to various failure conditions.

@startuml Error_Handling
state "Nominal" as nominal {
  state "Update GNSS"
  state "Update Sensors"
  state "Print Status"
}

state "Error Detection" as error_detect {
  state "I2C Timeout" as i2c_err
  state "Sensor Fault" as sensor_err
  state "No GNSS Fix" as gnss_err
  state "Watchdog Timeout" as watchdog_err
}

state "Recovery" as recovery {
  state "Retry I2C"
  state "Mark Sensor Invalid"
  state "Alert Watchdog"
  state "Log Error Code"
}

state "Safe Mode" as safe_mode {
  state "Continue with available sensors"
}

nominal --> error_detect: Detect anomaly

error_detect --> i2c_err
error_detect --> sensor_err
error_detect --> gnss_err
error_detect --> watchdog_err

i2c_err --> recovery: Attempt retry
sensor_err --> recovery: Disable sensor
gnss_err --> recovery: Continue (position optional)
watchdog_err --> recovery: Reset watchdog

recovery --> safe_mode: Mitigation complete
safe_mode --> nominal: Resume operation

note right of error_detect
  Errors detected via:
  - Timeout (I2C, GNSS read)
  - CRC failure (NMEA checksum)
  - Data out of range (pressure, temp)
  - Watchdog not responding
end note

note right of safe_mode
  System continues operating
  with degraded functionality
  Status shows "Invalid" for
  failed sensors
end note

@enduml

docs.title: Explanation:
docs.text:
• Nominal Operation: Normal GNSS/sensor update and display cycle
  • Error Detection: I2C timeouts, sensor faults, GNSS loss, watchdog unresponsive
  • Recovery Mechanisms: Retry I2C, mark sensors invalid, alert external system
  • Safe Mode: Continue with available sensors rather than crashing
  • Logging: Errors recorded for post-flight analysis

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
These diagrams provide a complete visual reference for understanding the SAFARI ADCS firmware:

  1. Architecture – System organization and module interactions
  2. Class Hierarchy – Sensor polymorphism and type relationships
  3. Data Flow – GNSS parsing pipeline and data processing
  4. Main Loop – Timing and execution of core operations
  5. I2C Topology – Physical device connections and addresses
  6. Status Display – Serial console output format
  7. Sensor Updates – Sequential sensor read operations
  8. Watchdog – External system communication protocol
  9. Initialization – Boot-up sequence and checks
  10. Error Handling – Fault detection and recovery strategies

Refer to these diagrams when: - Onboarding new developers - Understanding data flow for debugging - Planning sensor integration - Verifying timing requirements - Documenting communication protocols

docs.title: NDVI/NDRE Documentation
docs.text:
This documentation covers the full procedure for determining vegetation indices using a Sony A7S II converted to full-spectrum photography with a Hoya A25 red filter.

The methodology is based on Patón (2020), Normalized Difference Vegetation Index Determination in Urban Areas by Full-Spectrum Photography, Ecologies 1, 22–35, adapted for the Sony A7S II.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Rather than relying on expensive multispectral cameras, this procedure converts a consumer camera into a full-spectrum device capable of capturing red and near-infrared radiation simultaneously. A key feature of this methodology is that the spectral response of the specific camera body is characterized empirically from the calibration data itself, rather than assumed from manufacturer specifications.

Depending on the results of the wavelength characterization, the procedure produces either:

  • NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) — standard index using red and NIR bands
  • NDRE (Normalized Difference Red Edge) — more sensitive index using red-edge and NIR bands, preferred for detecting early plant stress and canopy nitrogen content

docs.title: Equipment
docs.text:
• Sony A7S II (full-spectrum converted)
  • Hoya A25 red filter
  • Macbeth X-Rite ColorChecker (11" × 8.25")
  • Computer running R, ImageJ, DCRAW, and ImageMagick

docs.title: Documentation Structure
docs.text:
• Camera Setup — conversion and base settings
  • Calibration Procedure — step-by-step calibration workflow
  • Index Selection — wavelength characterization and index decision logic

docs.title: Reference Data Preparation
docs.text:
Before collecting calibration photos, download the X-Rite ColorChecker spectral reflectance data from Ritchie et al. (2008), which covers all 24 panels at wavelengths from 400–900 nm including the NIR range required for this calibration. This published data is the same for all Macbeth ColorChecker devices as they are factory calibrated.

This data forms the Y variable (known reflectance) in both the wavelength characterization and the regression models.

docs.title: Photography Procedure
docs.text:
Place the Macbeth ColorChecker (11" × 8.25") flat on the ground facing incident light to avoid shadows. Position the camera 1 metre from the checker. Perform Custom White Balance before beginning.

Collect a minimum of 23 photographs covering the following range of conditions:

Parameter Range Time of day 10am–6pm F-stop f/4–f/16 Shutter speed 1/500s–1/6s Focal length Full range of your lens

This range ensures the regression models are valid across the full variety of conditions encountered in field use. Vary one parameter at a time where possible to ensure adequate coverage of the parameter space.

Check the histogram after every shot and reject any underexposed or overexposed frames — see White Balance.

docs.title: RAW Conversion
docs.text:
Convert all ARW files to 16-bit linear TIFF using DCRAW. This preserves the exact quantitative sensor data without any post-processing, gamma correction, or white balance adjustment applied by the camera:

dcraw -v -r 1 1 1 1 -M -o 0 -q 3 -W -T -4 -g 1 1 -t 0 *.ARW

The -r 1 1 1 1 flag is critical — it disables automatic channel weighting. The -4 flag outputs 16-bit linear data. Do not use any other conversion settings as they will alter the linear relationship between sensor input and output that the regression models depend on.

docs.title: Histogram Equalization
docs.text:
Equalize all TIFF files to expand the brightness range to maximum amplitude, eliminating residual exposure differences between photographs:

for f in $(ls *.tif); do convert $f -auto-level $f; done

On Windows PowerShell:

Get-ChildItem *.tif | ForEach-Object { magick $_.Name -auto-level $_.Name }

docs.title: ImageJ Measurement
docs.text:
Open each equalized TIFF in ImageJ and measure the mean brightness of each of the 24 ColorChecker panels in the R, G, and B channels:

  1. Open the TIFF file in ImageJ
  2. Go to Image → Color → Split Channels to separate R, G, B
  3. For each channel, use the rectangular selection tool to select each panel
  4. Record the mean brightness value from Analyze → Measure

Record results in a CSV with the following structure:

photo_id, time, fstop, speed, focal_length, panel_id, R_brightness, G_brightness, B_brightness

Target approximately 552 data points in total (23 photographs × 24 panels). This dataset feeds directly into both the wavelength characterization and the regression models.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
After hot mirror removal, the sensor receives red light approximately 100× more abundantly than NIR radiation. Without correction, the red channel saturates completely and the NIR channels are underexposed. Custom white balance (CWB) equalizes the three channels before each shooting session by using a fully overexposed sky reference frame.

CWB also prevents vignetting — the differential brightness between the centre and periphery of the image that would otherwise introduce spatial variation into the measurements.

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
Perform this at the start of every shooting session and repeat whenever lighting conditions change significantly (e.g. moving between open sky and under tree canopy, or when cloud cover changes).

  1. Mount the Hoya A25 filter on the lens
  2. Point the camera directly at open sky — avoid pointing at the sun
  3. Set aperture to maximum (lowest f-number available, f/4 on most lenses)
  4. Set shutter speed to approximately 30 seconds
  5. Take the photograph — all three channels should be fully saturated (pure white)
  6. Set this frame as the custom white balance reference in the camera menu

docs.title: Histogram Checking
docs.text:
After each calibration photograph, verify exposure using the histogram:

  • Correctly exposed: histogram peaks in the centre with no clipping at either end
  • Underexposed: histogram pushed to the left — increase exposure or open aperture
  • Overexposed: histogram pushed hard against the right — reduce exposure or close aperture

Reject and reshoot any photograph showing significant under or overexposure. Do not attempt to correct exposure in post-processing — the RAW conversion step must preserve the original linear sensor data.

Warning

Having removed the hot mirror, the risk of overexposure is substantially higher than with a standard camera. Always check the histogram after every shot, particularly when shooting in bright midday conditions.

docs.title: ImageJ Scripts Reference
docs.text: This page is a quick reference for both scripts with placeholder coefficient locations clearly marked. For the full decision logic on which script to use, see Index Selection.

docs.title: Your Calibration Coefficients
docs.text:
Record your coefficients here after completing the Regression Models step:

Model a b Red (R) NIR1 / Red-edge (G) NIR2 / NIR (B)

docs.title: Applying the Scripts
docs.text:
1. Open your equalized TIFF field photograph in ImageJ
  2. Go to Plugins → Macros → Run
  3. Select the appropriate script file
  4. The output TIFF will be saved to /tmp/ — move it to your working directory after running

docs.title: Batch Processing
docs.text:
To process multiple photographs, wrap either script in a batch loop using ImageJ's built-in batch processing:

  1. Go to Process → Batch → Macro
  2. Set input folder to your equalized TIFFs
  3. Set output folder for your results
  4. Paste the script content into the macro window
  5. Click Process

This applies the script to every TIFF in the input folder automatically.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Scripts output 32-bit TIFF files — preserve this bit depth throughout analysis
  • The NDVI_colors lookup table must be installed in ImageJ before running — it is included in the standard ImageJ distribution
  • Coefficient values are camera-specific and must be recalculated if a different camera body or a different conversion is used

docs.title: Decision Logic
docs.text:
The vegetation index used depends on the outcome of the Wavelength Characterization step and the R² values from the Regression Models step. Use this table to determine which script to use:

Green peak Blue R² Blue peak Index Channels 700–750 nm ≥0.85 ≥800 nm NDRE Green = red-edge, Blue = NIR >750 nm ≥0.85 ≥800 nm NDVI Red = R, Green = NIR Any <0.85 Any NDVI Red = R, Green = NIR only

docs.title: NDRE Script
docs.text:
Use when the green channel peak falls in the red-edge window (700–750 nm) and the blue channel is reliable.

NDRE = (NIR − RedEdge) / (NIR + RedEdge)

Replace [a_R], [b_R], [a_NIR1], [b_NIR1], [a_NIR2], [b_NIR2] with your coefficients from the regression step.

run("32-bit");
saveAs("Tiff", "/tmp/test.tif");
run("Split Channels");

selectWindow("C3-test.tif");
run("Close");

// Red channel → R reflectance (retained for reference)
selectWindow("C1-test.tif");
run("Multiply...", "value=[b_R]");
run("Exp");
run("Multiply...", "value=[a_R]");
saveAs("Tiff", "/tmp/R.tif");

// Green channel → red-edge reflectance
selectWindow("C2-test.tif");
run("Multiply...", "value=[b_NIR1]");
run("Exp");
run("Multiply...", "value=[a_NIR1]");
saveAs("Tiff", "/tmp/RE.tif");

// Blue channel → NIR reflectance
selectWindow("C3-test.tif");
run("Multiply...", "value=[b_NIR2]");
run("Exp");
run("Multiply...", "value=[a_NIR2]");
saveAs("Tiff", "/tmp/NIR.tif");

// NDRE = (NIR - RE) / (NIR + RE)
imageCalculator("Subtract create 32-bit", "NIR.tif", "RE.tif");
saveAs("Tiff", "/tmp/N1.tif");
imageCalculator("Add create 32-bit", "NIR.tif", "RE.tif");
saveAs("Tiff", "/tmp/D1.tif");

selectWindow("R.tif");
close();
selectWindow("RE.tif");
close();
selectWindow("NIR.tif");
close();

imageCalculator("Divide create 32-bit", "N1.tif", "D1.tif");
selectWindow("Result of N1.tif");
run("NDVI_colors");
saveAs("Tiff", "/tmp/NDRE.tif");

selectWindow("D1.tif");
close();
selectWindow("N1.tif");
close();

docs.title: NDVI Script
docs.text:
Use when the green channel peak is above 750 nm, or when the blue channel is unreliable.

NDVI = (NIR − R) / (NIR + R)

Replace [a_R], [b_R], [a_NIR1], [b_NIR1] with your coefficients from the regression step.

run("32-bit");
saveAs("Tiff", "/tmp/test.tif");
run("Split Channels");

selectWindow("C3-test.tif");
run("Close");

// Red channel → R reflectance
selectWindow("C1-test.tif");
run("Multiply...", "value=[b_R]");
run("Exp");
run("Multiply...", "value=[a_R]");
saveAs("Tiff", "/tmp/R.tif");

// Green channel → NIR1 reflectance
selectWindow("C2-test.tif");
run("Multiply...", "value=[b_NIR1]");
run("Exp");
run("Multiply...", "value=[a_NIR1]");
saveAs("Tiff", "/tmp/IR1.tif");

// NDVI = (NIR1 - R) / (NIR1 + R)
imageCalculator("Subtract create 32-bit", "IR1.tif", "R.tif");
saveAs("Tiff", "/tmp/N1.tif");
imageCalculator("Add create 32-bit", "IR1.tif", "R.tif");
saveAs("Tiff", "/tmp/D1.tif");

selectWindow("R.tif");
close();
selectWindow("IR1.tif");
close();

imageCalculator("Divide create 32-bit", "N1.tif", "D1.tif");
selectWindow("Result of N1.tif");
run("NDVI_colors");
saveAs("Tiff", "/tmp/NDVI.tif");

selectWindow("D1.tif");
close();
selectWindow("N1.tif");
close();

docs.title: Colour Scale
docs.text:
In both scripts, run("NDVI_colors") applies the standard vegetation index colour scale:

NDVI/NDRE value Colour Interpretation Close to −1 Black Water, deep shadow −1 to 0 Grey tones Non-photosynthetic surfaces (soil, rock, trunks, sky) 0 to ~0.3 Blue Low photosynthetic activity (lichens, algae, sparse vegetation) ~0.3 to ~0.6 Green-yellow Moderate photosynthetic activity (grass, shrubs) Close to 1 Red High photosynthetic activity (dense healthy vegetation)

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
The calibration procedure establishes the mathematical relationship between the brightness values recorded by the camera sensor and the true reflectance values of surfaces in red and near-infrared wavelengths. These relationships are expressed as exponential regression models of the form:

Reflectance = a × exp(b × Brightness)

One model is fitted for each reliable channel. The coefficients a and b are specific to this camera body and must be derived from your own calibration data — the coefficients reported in Patón (2020) apply only to the Nikon D50 and cannot be reused.

docs.title: Additional Purpose: Wavelength Characterization
docs.text: Because Sony does not publish the spectral sensitivity curve for the A7S II sensor, this calibration procedure also characterizes which wavelengths each channel is capturing empirically. This is done by correlating measured channel brightnesses against the known reflectance values of the ColorChecker panels at each candidate wavelength. The result determines which vegetation index is most appropriate for this camera.

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. White Balance — custom white balance before each session
  2. ColorChecker Photography — collecting calibration photos
  3. RAW Conversion — converting ARW files to TIFF
  4. Histogram Equalization — normalizing exposure
  5. ImageJ Measurement — measuring panel brightnesses
  6. Wavelength Characterization — determining channel wavelengths
  7. Regression Models — fitting reflectance models
  8. Validation — hierarchical partitioning validation

docs.title: Software Requirements
docs.text: Software Purpose Source DCRAW 9.20 ARW to TIFF conversion dcraw.sourceforge.net ImageMagick 7+ Histogram equalization imagemagick.org ImageJ 1.8.0+ Brightness measurement imagej.nih.gov R 4.0+ Regression and validation r-project.org R package: hier.part Hierarchical partitioning CRAN

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Three exponential regression models are fitted to relate the brightness values measured by the camera sensor to the true reflectance values of the ColorChecker panels. These models are used in the ImageJ script to convert raw channel brightness in field photographs into reflectance values, from which the vegetation index is calculated.

The models take the form:

Reflectance = a × exp(b × Brightness)

docs.title: R Code
docs.text:
# Load data
your_data <- read.csv("imagej_measurements.csv")

# Merge with X-Rite reflectance values
xrite <- read.csv("xrite_nir_reflectances.csv")
your_data <- merge(your_data, xrite, by = "panel_id")

# Red channel → R reflectance
model_R <- nls(reflectance_R ~ a * exp(b * R_brightness),
               data = your_data,
               start = list(a = 0.02, b = 0.00005))

# Green channel → NIR1 reflectance
model_NIR1 <- nls(reflectance_NIR1 ~ a * exp(b * G_brightness),
                  data = your_data,
                  start = list(a = 0.03, b = 0.00005))

# Blue channel → NIR2 reflectance
# Include regardless — reliability determined by wavelength characterization
model_NIR2 <- nls(reflectance_NIR2 ~ a * exp(b * B_brightness),
                  data = your_data,
                  start = list(a = 0.05, b = 0.00005))

summary(model_R)
summary(model_NIR1)
summary(model_NIR2)

docs.title: Extracting Coefficients
docs.text:
# Extract a and b coefficients for use in ImageJ script
coef_R    <- coef(model_R)
coef_NIR1 <- coef(model_NIR1)
coef_NIR2 <- coef(model_NIR2)

cat("Red channel:   a =", coef_R["a"],    "  b =", coef_R["b"],    "\n")
cat("NIR1 channel:  a =", coef_NIR1["a"], "  b =", coef_NIR1["b"], "\n")
cat("NIR2 channel:  a =", coef_NIR2["a"], "  b =", coef_NIR2["b"], "\n")

Record these coefficients — they are entered into the ImageJ script in the Index Selection step.

docs.title: Acceptance Criteria
docs.text:
Model Minimum R² Notes Red (R) 0.95 Should be cleanly fitted — red channel is least ambiguous NIR1 (G) 0.95 Should be comparably clean NIR2 (B) 0.85 Lower threshold reflects greater uncertainty in blue channel

If R or NIR1 fall below their thresholds, the most common cause is inconsistent CWB between sessions. Identify and reshoot the sessions where CWB was not properly set and refit the models.

If NIR2 falls below 0.85, treat the blue channel as unreliable regardless of the wavelength characterization result and fall back to standard NDVI using R and NIR1 only.

Warning

Do not use the coefficients from Patón (2020). Those values apply only to the Nikon D50 and will produce incorrect reflectance estimates on the A7S II.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Hierarchical Partitioning (HP) analysis tests whether any of the experimental factors — time of day, focal length, f-stop, and shutter speed — significantly explain the residuals of the regression models. If a factor is significant it means the calibration is not fully accounting for variation introduced by that shooting condition, and the regression coefficients will not be reliable across the full range of field conditions.

HP is used rather than standard ANOVA because the residuals are unlikely to meet the normality and variance homogeneity requirements of parametric analysis. HP uses a randomization test and shows the percentage of independent effect of each factor on the residuals.

docs.title: R Code
docs.text:
library(hier.part)

# Prepare factor variables from your calibration data
factors <- data.frame(
  time         = your_data$time,
  focal_length = your_data$focal_length,
  fstop        = your_data$fstop,
  speed        = your_data$speed
)

# Validate each model
cat("=== Red channel residuals ===\n")
hier.part(residuals(model_R), factors)

cat("\n=== NIR1 channel residuals ===\n")
hier.part(residuals(model_NIR1), factors)

cat("\n=== NIR2 channel residuals ===\n")
hier.part(residuals(model_NIR2), factors)

docs.title: Interpreting the Output
docs.text:
For each model, HP reports the percentage of variance in the residuals attributable to each factor, along with a significance test. The target outcome is that no factor is significant for any model.

Outcome Meaning Action No factors significant Calibration is robust across all tested conditions Proceed to index selection Time significant Spectral composition of light varying with time of day not fully corrected by CWB Tighten CWB procedure and reshoot F-stop significant Exposure variation not fully corrected Check histogram equalization step Shutter speed significant As above Check histogram equalization step Focal length significant Vignetting not fully corrected Check CWB overexposure reference frame

docs.title: Reshoot Criteria
docs.text: If any factor is significant, identify the sessions contributing most to that factor's effect and reshoot them with more careful CWB and histogram checking. A single poorly controlled session can drive significance across all models. Refit the models and rerun HP after reshooting.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Because Sony does not publish the spectral sensitivity curve for the A7S II sensor, the dominant wavelength captured by each channel is determined empirically from the calibration data. This is done by correlating the measured channel brightnesses across the 24 ColorChecker panels against the known panel reflectances at each candidate wavelength. The wavelength producing the highest correlation for a given channel is its dominant wavelength.

This step also determines the reliability of the blue channel for NIR2 measurement, and from that decides which vegetation index — NDVI or NDRE — is most appropriate for this camera body.

docs.title: R Code
docs.text:
library(dplyr)

# Load X-Rite reflectance data (columns named nm_600, nm_610, ... nm_950)
reflectance_table <- read.csv("xrite_nir_reflectances.csv")

# Load ImageJ brightness measurements
brightness <- read.csv("imagej_measurements.csv")

# Candidate wavelengths to test
wavelengths <- seq(600, 950, by = 10)

# Correlate each channel's brightness against known reflectance at each wavelength
characterize_channel <- function(channel_brightness, reflectance_table) {
  correlations <- sapply(wavelengths, function(wl) {
    col_name <- paste0("nm_", wl)
    cor(channel_brightness, reflectance_table[[col_name]])
  })
  data.frame(wavelength = wavelengths, correlation = correlations)
}

red_char   <- characterize_channel(brightness$R_brightness, reflectance_table)
green_char <- characterize_channel(brightness$G_brightness, reflectance_table)
blue_char  <- characterize_channel(brightness$B_brightness, reflectance_table)

# Extract peak wavelengths and blue channel reliability
red_peak_wl   <- red_char$wavelength[which.max(red_char$correlation)]
green_peak_wl <- green_char$wavelength[which.max(green_char$correlation)]
blue_peak_wl  <- blue_char$wavelength[which.max(blue_char$correlation)]
blue_max_cor  <- max(blue_char$correlation)

cat("Red channel dominant wavelength:  ", red_peak_wl, "nm\n")
cat("Green channel dominant wavelength:", green_peak_wl, "nm\n")
cat("Blue channel dominant wavelength: ", blue_peak_wl, "nm\n")
cat("Blue channel peak correlation:    ", round(blue_max_cor, 3), "\n\n")

# Determine index recommendation
blue_reliable <- blue_max_cor >= 0.85 & blue_peak_wl >= 800
green_rededge <- green_peak_wl >= 700 & green_peak_wl <= 750

if (blue_reliable & green_rededge) {
  cat("RESULT: Green channel = red-edge (~", green_peak_wl, "nm)\n")
  cat("        Blue channel  = NIR      (~", blue_peak_wl, "nm)\n")
  cat("RECOMMENDED INDEX: NDRE = (NIR2 - NIR1) / (NIR2 + NIR1)\n")
} else if (blue_reliable & !green_rededge) {
  cat("RESULT: Green channel = NIR1 (~", green_peak_wl, "nm)\n")
  cat("        Blue channel  = NIR2 (~", blue_peak_wl, "nm)\n")
  cat("RECOMMENDED INDEX: NDVI = (NIR1 - R) / (NIR1 + R)\n")
  cat("        Green channel too deep for red-edge use\n")
} else {
  cat("RESULT: Green channel = NIR1 (~", green_peak_wl, "nm)\n")
  cat("        Blue channel unreliable — exclude from analysis\n")
  cat("RECOMMENDED INDEX: NDVI = (NIR1 - R) / (NIR1 + R)\n")
}

docs.title: Interpreting the Output
docs.text: Green peak Blue reliable Blue peak Recommended index 700–750 nm Yes (≥0.85) ≥800 nm NDRE — green = red-edge, blue = NIR >750 nm Yes (≥0.85) ≥800 nm NDVI — green = NIR, blue = NIR2 Any No (<0.85) Any NDVI — red and green channels only

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: This characterization identifies the dominant wavelength per channel but does not resolve the full bandwidth. For NDVI and NDRE calculation this is sufficient — what matters is that red is capturing the chlorophyll absorption region (~650–680 nm) and that the NIR or red-edge channel is clearly separated from it. Proceed to Regression Models regardless of the outcome — the characterization determines how you interpret and use the models, not whether you fit them.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Sony A7S II contains an internal hot mirror filter (also called an IR-cut filter) that blocks near-infrared radiation to produce images similar to human vision. For full-spectrum photography, this filter must be removed and replaced with a piece of optically clear glass of the same dimensions.

Once removed, the sensor becomes sensitive to UV, visible, and NIR wavelengths simultaneously. The Hoya A25 red filter is then used externally on the lens to block UV, blue, and green wavelengths, leaving only red and NIR radiation to reach the sensor.

docs.title: Why Professional Conversion is Required
docs.text: Unlike the Nikon D50 used in Patón (2020), the hot mirror in the Sony A7S II is integrated into the sensor stack rather than sitting as a discrete removable piece. DIY removal risks permanent sensor damage. Professional conversion services have the tools and experience to perform this safely and recalibrate back-focus after the conversion.

docs.title: Recommended Services
docs.text:
• Kolari Vision — kolariVision.com
  • LifePixel — lifepixel.com

Request a full-spectrum conversion specifically — not an IR-only conversion, which permanently limits the camera to infrared wavelengths only. Full-spectrum preserves the ability to use external filters to select different wavelength ranges.

docs.title: What to Expect After Conversion
docs.text:
• The camera will no longer produce accurate colours without an external filter
  • The metering system will be unreliable — manual exposure control is required for all shooting
  • Back-focus will be recalibrated by the conversion service so lenses focus correctly
  • NIR light focuses at a slightly different point than visible light — some chromatic aberration is normal without filtration

docs.title: Base Settings
docs.text: Parameter Value Reason ISO 100 Base ISO minimises noise and overexposure risk Format ARW (RAW uncompressed) Preserves linear sensor data without post-processing Mode Manual Metering system unreliable after hot mirror removal White balance Custom (CWB) Set before each session — see White Balance

docs.title: Filter
docs.text:
Mount the Hoya A25 red filter on the lens. This filter transmits approximately 95% of red and NIR wavelengths while blocking UV, blue, and green. With the hot mirror removed and the A25 filter in place, the three sensor channels capture:

Channel Captures Role Red ~600–700 nm Red reflectance (R) Green NIR1 — exact wavelength determined by calibration Red-edge or NIR1 Blue NIR2 — exact wavelength determined by calibration NIR2 if reliable

Warning

The exact wavelengths captured by the green and blue channels depend on your specific camera body and cannot be assumed from manufacturer specifications. The Wavelength Characterization step determines these empirically from your calibration data.

docs.title: Exposure Range
docs.text:
Due to hot mirror removal, the sensor receives significantly more light than normal. Finding the correct exposure requires initial testing. The following ranges have been found to work as a starting point:

Parameter Range Exposure (Ev) 6.1–14.3 F-stop f/4–f/16 Shutter speed 1/500s–1/6s

Apertures below f/4 tend to overexpose. Shutter speeds faster than 1/500s do not allow enough infrared radiation. Always verify exposure using the histogram — see White Balance for histogram checking procedure.

docs.title: EEPROM
docs.text:
Each CaribouLite is pre-configured by the contract manufacturer before shipping. The Full and the ISM versions are further distinguishable by the configuration resistors placed on the back side of the board (marked as "CFG/1234").

CaribouLite doesn't have an FPGA configuration flash device, as the ICE40 device is dynamically configured by the RPI quickly and on-demand. It rather has a general board configuration device (EEPROM) as required by RPi's HAT device rules.

docs.title: EEPROM Data Structure
docs.text:
The code is located here and is based on RPi EERPROM Utils Tools provided by RaspberryPi. The ID EEPROM internal structure is described here.

The general structure is as follows: 1. HEADER: containing a valid header key (SIGN), its version, the number of ATOMs that follow the header and the total size of the EEPROM contents. 2. VENDOR INFO: Product information and Vendor information, and the product UID (unique 128bit identifier / serial number). 3. GPIO MAP: a listing of CaribouLite's RPi GPIO states and flags, for each GPIO pin (GPIO2-GPIO27). 4. DEVICE TREE: as the system is booting, the RPi kernel automatically reads this ATOM from the EEPROM and sets it as an overlay. As a result, the kernel modules needed for CaribouLite to operate (SMI, SPI, etc.) are probed on startup and configured according to the overlay. 5. PROPRIETARY DATA: this part contains additional configuration information for the board - I/Q imbalance calibration values, Tx-Power calibration, TCXO calibration, GPIO and PMOD states, etc. Some of these values shall be programmed by the CM, others are just place-holders for the user to better organize his work.

docs.title: Gracefully Accessing Data
docs.text:
To access the EEPROM data, one can use our code which also validates and decodes the information. The easier way to access the information within the programmed EEPROM is to use Raspbian's Linux sysfs as follows:

cd /proc/device-tree/hat/

# name
cat name

# product: "CaribouLite RPI Hat"
cat product

# product_id: "0x0001"
cat product_id

# product_ver: "0x0001"
cat product_ver

# uuid: "AAAAAAAA-BBBB-CCCC-DDDD-EEEEEEEEEEEE" GUID format
#       which is also the board's raw serial number
cat uuid

# vendor: "CaribouLabs.co"
cat vendor

docs.title: Reprogramming the EEPROM
docs.text: Reprogramming the EEPROM is possible using our EEPROM utilities. More info - coming soon.
